seasonal_wine.name: Winter pruning, January
seasonal_wine.description: The coldest months are spent pruning the terraced vines and the valley falls quiet. Cellars are busy racking the previous year's reds, and it is the calmest time to visit the Gaia lodges without harvest-season crowds.
seasonal_wine.tip: Quintas are at their quietest now; private tastings are easy to book and the terraced hillsides under low winter light are starkly beautiful.

seasonal_wine.name: Essencia do Vinho, late February
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, last weekend of February into early March
seasonal_wine.description: Portugal's flagship wine tasting fills the Palacio da Bolsa in Porto with around 4,000 wines from more than 400 producers. Douro reds, whites and Ports pour alongside the rest of the country across four days of tastings and masterclasses.
seasonal_wine.tip: Saturday is the busiest and priciest day; weekday afternoons give you more room at the producer tables.

seasonal_wine.name: Bud break, March
seasonal_wine.description: The first green shoots break on the terraces as spring arrives. Almond and cherry blossom dot the lower valley, and new-vintage white and rose releases begin to reach shelves as the cellars finish the previous harvest's whites.
seasonal_wine.tip: The valley is green and uncrowded in March; pair vineyard walks with the early new-vintage white releases.

seasonal_wine.name: Spring growth and terrace walks, April
seasonal_wine.description: Canopies fill out across the terraces and the river runs high with spring rain. It is one of the best months for hiking the vineyard trails and combining estate visits with mild weather before the summer heat sets in.
seasonal_wine.tip: Book a terrace-walk-plus-tasting combination now; the heat of July and August makes midday vineyard walking far harder.

seasonal_wine.name: Flowering, May
seasonal_wine.description: The vines flower in late spring, the moment that sets the size of the coming crop. The valley is lush and the river warm enough for boat trips, making it a popular and increasingly busy time to visit before the summer peak.
seasonal_wine.tip: Combine a Douro river cruise with a riverside quinta tasting; the water is the classic way to read the terraced landscape.

seasonal_wine.name: Sao Joao and early summer, June
seasonal_wine.description: Porto erupts for the Festa de Sao Joao on the night of 23 June, an all-night street party of grilled sardines, bonfires and Vinho Verde and Douro wine. In the valley the vines are setting fruit and the long summer ripening begins.
seasonal_wine.tip: Base in Porto for Sao Joao on 23 June, then drive up to the valley the next day to recover among the quiet terraces.

seasonal_wine.name: High summer and veraison, July
seasonal_wine.description: Temperatures soar in the sheltered valley and the grapes begin to change colour at veraison. Tastings move indoors to cool cellars and shaded terraces, and the estates plan harvest dates as the fruit ripens fast.
seasonal_wine.tip: Ask for white Port and tonic, the local summer aperitif; it is how the valley drinks through the July heat.

seasonal_wine.name: Pre-harvest preparation, August
seasonal_wine.description: The valley readies its lagares and presses as ripening accelerates. Quintas confirm picking teams and harvest-experience bookings fill up fast for September. The river is at its warmest and the terraces shimmer in the heat.
seasonal_wine.tip: Lock in any harvest-experience booking before the end of August; September dates at the popular quintas sell out early.

seasonal_wine.name: The vindima harvest, September
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, mid September to early October
seasonal_wine.description: The vindima is the heart of the Douro year. Pickers fill the terraces and grapes are foot-trodden in granite lagares late into the night to traditional music. Many quintas open the harvest to guests for picking, treading and feasting.
what_to_taste: Fermenting must straight from the lagar
seasonal_wine.tip: The lagarada foot-treading happens after dark; choose a quinta that still treads by foot, such as those running traditional harvest experiences, for the full ritual.

seasonal_wine.name: Late harvest and autumn colour, October
seasonal_wine.description: Higher and later sites finish picking as the terraces turn gold and russet. The light is at its best for photography, the crowds thin and cellars hum with fermenting wine. It is one of the most rewarding months to visit the valley.
seasonal_wine.tip: Mid October offers the autumn colour without the harvest-tour crush of late September; many quintas still let you watch fermentation in progress.

seasonal_wine.name: New wine and magusto, November
seasonal_wine.description: The first young reds of the vintage appear and the Sao Martinho chestnut-roasting magusto tradition brings agua-pe and jeropiga, the rustic part-fermented drinks of the harvest. The cellars settle into the long work of ageing the new wine.
seasonal_wine.tip: Around Sao Martinho on 11 November, look for agua-pe and roast chestnuts at village tascas, the homespun side of the Douro harvest.

seasonal_wine.name: Vintage Port declarations, December
seasonal_wine.description: In outstanding years the major houses declare a vintage Port roughly two years after the harvest, the highest expression of the Douro's fortified tradition. The festive season also pours aged tawny, the classic winter Port for cheese and dessert.
seasonal_wine.tip: A vintage is only declared in the finest years; in undeclared years the houses release single-quinta vintage Ports worth seeking out instead.
